bio:
Helping you unlock your potential through masterful communication 🚀
Free 2 Hour Training 👇
descripcion:
Never answer a question quickly.

When someone asks you something, the worst thing you can do is rush straight into your answer. Because when you rush, you look nervous. When you look nervous, you lose credibility. And when you lose credibility, your answer doesn't land the way it should.

Here's what to do instead: pause. Take a breath. Let the question settle for just a second or two. That pause does three things. First, it gives you time to organise your thoughts. Second, it signals confidence because confident people don't need to fill every silence. Third, it makes the other person feel heard because you're actually considering what they asked.

Most people think pausing makes them look slow or unsure. It's the opposite. The pause makes you look thoughtful, controlled, and present.

Next time someone asks you a question, try this. Pause. Breathe. Then answer. You'll notice the difference immediately, and so will they.

bio:

descripcion:
I was SO CLOSE! 😬

Comment 'CHAT' if you want my free 2 hour training to stop rambling and speak more clearly.

These are the tips I shared: 
 
1. The voice you have right now isn't your natural voice it's your habitual voice.

2. People form beliefs about you not just based on how you look, but also how you sound. You can buy the way you look, but you can't buy the way you sound. That's earnt.

3. Stop talking at the same speed. Slow down when saying something important, otherwise speed up.

4. Don't rush to answer a question. Pause first - it makes you sound more thoughtful.

ecord a video of yourself speaking for 5 minutes and review it- self awareness is the first step to improving the way you speak

6. End every sentences on a low pitch, it makes you sound more certain.

7. Your posture introduces you before your words do.

8. You become what you practice.

9. Match the expression on your face with the emotion behind your words.

10. Stop trying to be interesting. Learn how to be more interested.

11. Don't report your stories, relive them.

12. Stop replying to people with Yes But - start saying YES AND

13. What you don't change about your communication skills, you are choosing...

14. Your voice is an instrument - you don't use an instrument, you PLAY an instrument - so learn how to play it.

15. Learn how to be vulnerable without bringing the mood down.

Which one's your favorite tip?!

descripcion:
These speaking habits ruin your credibility!

Upspeak: When you end your sentences on a high pitch - don't do this it makes you sound less certain!

Vocal fry: When you don't have enough breath support when you speak - it makes you sound croaky and low energy.

Speaking too fast: Often a symptom of nervousness - practice consciously slowing down when you speak.

Monotone: When your delivery is FLAT. This makes you less engaging and hard to listen to.

Singsong: When your voice follows a pattern of speaking that is repetitive. Think news anchors, telemarketers, etc. The moment your brain can predict how something sounds, it switches off.

What speaking habits do you need to fix?

descripcion:
Most people think pausing makes them look uncertain.

The opposite is true.

When you pause, you give your words weight. You let the listener catch up. You show you're in control of the room, not racing to fill silence because you're afraid of it.

Here's the thing though: if you pause and your body language screams discomfort, the pause will feel awkward. But if you pause and stay grounded, shoulders back, eyes steady, the pause becomes powerful.

Try this: next time you're speaking, finish a thought, then pause for two full seconds before moving on. Don't fill it. el like what you're saying actually matters.

descripcion:
When you master the comfortable pause, something remarkable happens. Your ums and ahs vanish completely. These filler words destroy your authority faster than any mistake you could make.

Think of it like this: you're playing a beautiful piano piece, then suddenly hit a jarring wrong note. That's exactly what "um" does to your message. It breaks the flow, shatters credibility, and makes your audience question your expertise.

The solution isn't speaking faster or preparing more. It's getting comfortable with silence. When you feel that urge to fill space with "um" or "ah," pause instead. Let the silence do the work.

Your audience needs those pauses to process what you're saying. You need them to gather your thoughts. Everyone wins when you embrace the pause.

What's one filler word you catch yourself using?

descripcion:
As I took over one international team after another, until it spanned across 17 countries, I knew I had two choices. 

Assume everyone communicates the way I do. Or get curious enough to find out how they actually do.

I chose curious.

I had 1:1 conversations with every single person on my team and asked them directly: In your culture, how do you receive feedback? How do you like to be held accountable? What does respect look like to you in a meeting?

No script. No HR framework. 

Just me, willing to look completely uncultured if it meant I actually understood the people I was leading.

I didn’t protect my ego. I protected the relationship.

Then I wrote it down. Each person’s top 3 communication preferences in a notepad I kept specifically for our 1:1s. 

So when I sat across from them, I wasn’t guessing. I was showing up prepared to lead them.

For group meetings, I built a separate list. What communication styles had enough overlap to work for the room. What didn’t. 

And I was upfront with the team about it. Here’s how we’re going to operate together, here’s why, and here’s what I need from each of you.

When people feel seen — not managed, not processed, but actually seen — they don’t look for the exit. They go all in.

One after the other, my teams became the highest-performing in the organization. And I became the leader they couldn’t afford to lose.

#leadershipcommunication

It took me 10 years to figure out why my degrees, diplomas, and expertise weren’t enough to get me in the right rooms and move the right people to action.

Influence.

Influence was the missing piece. And I’ll be honest with you. I thought I didn’t need influence.

My ego around my degrees and my diplomas was blinding me. I thought influence was something that people who didn’t have what I have, needed to succeed.

I thought of influence as a sleazy car salesman tactic.
I was embarrassingly wrong.

Influence is the ability to meet people where they are at, fill in the gaps, and give them the logical and emotional certainty they need to move forward with you.

And it can be learned.

That decade of paying the price — and then rebuilding from scratch — is what I’ve compressed into the Expert to Leader Challenge, starting next Monday.

If you’re technically sharp but not yet landing with the authority your results deserve, this is for you.

Comment EXPERT and I’ll send you the details.

descripcion:
Ridiculous because it’s so simple. Yet most people I worked with didn’t do it.

Because of ego.
Because they simply didn’t know.
But mostly because of ego.

I led teams across 17 countries. 13 of those teams became high-performing teams. 

One habit.

Here’s the big reveal: I operated with zero assumption.

Zero assumption of what my team understood.
Zero assumption of what people thought.
Zero assumption of their buy-in.

Most leaders operate on the belief that because they said it once, it was heard. 

Or because the strategy makes sense on paper, everyone is automatically aligned.

When I stripped away assumptions, I was forced to ask better questions. 

I was forced to communicate with absolute clarity.

 I had to stop hoping for mind-reading and start relying on dialogue.

You know what zero assumption creates? Safety.

Safety for my teams to say, “I don’t get it.” Safety to push back on a timeline. Safety to tell me when a plan is fundamentally flawed before we execute it.

If you are a senior leader ready to close the gap between your technical brilliance and your executive authority—without the guesswork—comment SPEAK and I’ll send you the details to apply for my mentoring program.

I read all applications and if selected, we’ll map out exactly how to build high-trust, high-impact teams and position yourself for the C-suite.

Start here👇
descripcion:
You know exactly what needs to be said. And when you walk into that room, you say approximately 60% of it.

You trim the part that might make them defensive. You soften the conclusion so it sounds like a suggestion. You frame the risk as a possibility instead of a certainty.

And you walk out of that meeting knowing, in your gut, that the message did not land the way it needed to.

This is not a courage problem. It is a communication pattern and psychology has a name for it.

It is called upward communication apprehension, and research consistently shows it is more pronounced in high performers than in average ones. 

The reason is counterintuitive: the more you understand the stakes, the more you self-censor. Your brain reads the power differential in the room, activates a threat response, and begins editing your message in real time to protect the relationship.

The result is what communication researchers call message dilution: the gap between what you know and what you actually say. And the higher you climb, the more expensive that gap becomes.

When you communicate upwards, you are not just delivering information. You are navigating a power dynamic in real time.

The shift is not about being more direct. It is about learning to deliver a full, unfiltered message in a way that the person above you can actually receive without triggering their defences, without damaging the relationship, and without softening the truth.

That is the communication skill that gets you into the C-suite. Not the ability to say hard things. The ability to say them in a way that lands.

Send this to somone who has the ambition to be a trusted and valued executive.

Most incredibly smart, highly capable leaders take the fact that they can speak for granted.

Because you can speak, you assume you are fluent in executive communication. They are not the same thing.

You spend years honing your technical expertise. You get sharper, faster, and more strategic. But you never actually practice articulating that thinking. 

You never train the specific skill of connecting the dots between what you know and how it drives the organization’s priorities forward in a room full of decision-makers.

So what happens?

You walk into a high-stakes meeting, open your mouth, and deliver a data dump. You sound like the expert, but you don’t sound like the authority.

And you wonder why you have no influence.

Communication at the senior level isn’t a natural byproduct of being smart. It is a distinct, trainable skill. It requires a map, a framework, and deliberate practice.

If you want the C-suite, you have to stop relying on the fact that you know how to talk, and start learning how to command the room.

descripcion:
I would never complain. Ever. I’m not a toddler having a tantrum for not getting a toy.

I need my boss to see me as a peer, not a subordinate begging for scraps.

Here’s exactly how I would handle it:

First, I would schedule a 15-minute meeting and say:

“I saw the announcement regarding the new structure. Congratulations on promoting [Name]. Moving forward, my priority is ensuring my output aligns with the organization and your highest objectives. Over the next 90 days, I’ll be reallocating my bandwidth to [high-impact project]. If there are specific metrics you need to see from me to secure the next level, would you be able to send them by Friday or Monday?”

Then I would stop talking.

Here is why this works:

The Law of Disdain. By briefly acknowledging the promotion and immediately pivoting to your own trajectory, you show that their decision didn’t break you. It is unsettling to people who expected you to beg.

The Illusion of Choice. You aren’t asking what you need to do. You are telling them what you are going to do. You give them the illusion of control by asking for metrics and by offering Friday or Monday. You win either way.

The Power of the Void. Silence is the ultimate weapon. Most people over-explain when they are nervous. By staying silent after your statement, you force your boss to fill the void and either reveal their true intentions or make concessions they never planned to make.

Stop begging for what you deserve. 
Start negotiating from a position of leverage.

#leadershipcommunication

descripcion:
Most employees don’t need more motivation from leadership.
They need more stability.
Because when teams feel emotionally scattered, performance usually follows right behind it.
The strongest leaders consistently provide three things:
Clarity.
People need to understand what actually matters without constantly interpreting shifting priorities.
Consistency.
Standards, communication, and accountability cannot change based on leadership moods or pressure.
Progress.
People disengage when work starts feeling directionless and growth disappears.
That’s what builds trust over time.
Not inspirational speeches.
Not hype.
Not leadership theater.
I’ve watched teams stay deeply committed through incredibly difficult business seasons because leadership maintained those three things consistently.
People can handle pressure far better than most leaders think.
What wears teams down is chaos, unpredictability, and emotional inconsistency.
Strong leadership is usually more repetitive than people realize:
clear direction,
steady behavior,
forward movement,
repeated long enough that teams begin trusting it. #leadership #leadershipskills #learnontiktok

descripcion:
(psst) Your manager isn't failing to see your potential... your 1:1s just aren't making it visible.
I've run 1:1s from both sides of this table. As a COO reporting to founders, and as a leader managing my own team.
The operators who advance quickly all share the same habit
(let me share it with you now!)
Most leaders show up, give updates, answer questions, and walk out thinking that was productive 🙃
But your manager is forming an opinion of you in every single meeting.
The 1:1 format you use shapes whether they trust you and see you moving up... or not.
👇 4 things to cover on your 1:1s every week:
1️⃣ Start with wins
→ One or two business outcomes you moved since the last check-in.
Not tasks. Outcomes.
(This is how credibility gets built. Quietly, week over week.)
2️⃣ Roadblocks you resolved without them
→ Problems you handled on your own.
This tells them you can carry the authority that comes with a bigger role.
3️⃣ What you need from them
→ Two or three specific asks, framed as "I need X to move Y forward by Z."
Shows you're thinking ahead, not waiting for direction.
4️⃣ Confirm priorities before you leave
→ Your top three for the week, and ask if anything needs to shift.
(They'll feel like they're leading a team that's actually moving together.)
Run this every week.
At some point your manager stops checking in on you and starts counting on you instead.
💻 Comment "NEWSLETTER" if you want to get leadership strategies and frameworks like this delivered to your inbox every week.
👉 Follow @growwithpolina for real-world leadership tools and strategies that actually work

descripcion:
If you’re a leader, here’s another cold hard truth bomb:

Sometimes the best thing you can do is leave.

Not because you’re weak.

Not because you’re giving up.

And not because leadership is hard.

Because you’ve exhausted your ability to create change.

You’ve communicated.

You’ve coached.

You’ve challenged.

You’ve provided solutions.

You’ve fought for the culture.

And nothing changes.

At some point, staying becomes enabling.

Sometimes the most powerful leadership statement you can make is:

👉 “This is not acceptable.”

And then having the courage to walk away.

People often discover the value of leadership after it’s gone.

Sometimes leadership means staying and fighting.

Sometimes leadership means leaving and showing a better way.

🚀 Join our leadership community:

descripcion:
The leaders who scare me most aren’t the ones who get challenged. They’re the ones who never do.Because when people stop disagreeing, stop speaking up, and stop bringing problems to leadership, it doesn’t mean everything is fine. It usually means they’ve learned it’s safer to stay quiet.

The best leaders don’t surround themselves with agreement. They surround themselves with truth. Even when it’s uncomfortable. Especially when it’s uncomfortable.

#Leadership #BridgeTheConflict #ConflictResolution #BusinessLeadership #CommunicationSkills
